summary: Self-taught American luminist painter of 19th-century frontier and river scenes. Best known for Fur Traders Descending the Missouri and a political trilogy (Stump Speaking, The County Election, and a third painting featuring a jovial man in three hats). Also painted Order No. 11, attacking General Ewing's forced depopulation of Missouri.

comprehensive_summary:
George Caleb Bingham (1811–1879) was a self-taught American luminist painter known for frontier and river scenes of 19th-century Missouri. He studied at the Dusseldorf Academy alongside Eastman Johnson and was a contemporary of genre painter William Sidney Mount.

His most famous painting is Fur Traders Descending the Missouri, originally titled French Trader-Half Breed Son before being renamed by the American Art-Union (founded 1839, with members including Emanuel Leutze, Asher Durand, and Thomas Cole), who feared the original title would cause offense. The painting shows a man in a Phrygian cap (liberty cap) wearing a pink-and-white striped shirt, a half-Native American boy leaning on pelts, and an animal often thought to be a cat but actually a bear cub (per the Met), all staring at the viewer. Bingham reworked it in 1851 as The Trappers' Return.

Bingham also created a political trilogy about small-town Missouri democracy: Stump Speaking, The County Election (1852), and a third painting featuring a jovial man wearing three hats. The County Election symbolizes chance through boys tossing a knife and a man flipping a coin, while a bandaged man represents underlying violence. Bingham mockingly gave E.D. Sappington, who had beaten him in an election, a shiny top hat. The series parallels William Hogarth's Elections paintings.

Other river scenes include The Jolly Flatboatmen (a man in a red shirt dancing on a ship), Jolly Flatboatmen in Port, Raftsmen Playing Cards (two men on a bench, one in blue jeans, while two men in green hats watch), and Shooting for the Beef. He also painted his own version of Washington Crossing the Delaware (with Washington sitting on a horse, unlike Leutze's standing version). His painting Order No. 11 attacks General Thomas Ewing's forced depopulation of part of Missouri, showing a family pleading with 'redleg' troops planning to burn their house. He also painted a portrait of Vinnie Ream playing a harp.
recursive_suggestions: Emanuel Leutze — Washington Crossing the Delaware; both in American Art-Union
William Sidney Mount — contemporary American genre painter
Thomas Cole — member of the American Art-Union
